summary: O'Neil argues that data, not money, is the true currency of the digital age, explaining how Google, Facebook, and Amazon dominate their markets by controlling vast datasets about users. She examines Amazon's new patent for selfie-based payment authentication as a strategic move to acquire facial recognition data that Facebook already possesses. The post connects this corporate data grab to broader surveillance implications, warning that widespread facial recognition could make location tracking ubiquitous. O'Neil concludes with a rare call to action, urging readers to resist this particular form of data collection before it becomes normalized.
key_points: Data is the true currency of the digital age - tech giants dominate not because of customer volume but because of data extraction capabilities
Google's data monopoly is genuinely anti-competitive because competitors cannot access equivalent user data, regardless of service quality
Amazon's selfie payment patent is a strategic play to acquire facial recognition data that currently only Facebook possesses
Facial recognition technology could eliminate the need for GPS tracking - faces alone would be sufficient to track people through video surveillance
Users actively help build these surveillance systems by labeling photos and providing training data
money_quotes: When you think of currency in the digital age, stop thinking money, not even Bitcoin. Start thinking about data.
We're not, we're just its product, or rather our data is.
It's not simply a question of how often we use their services, it's a question of how much data they can extract from us while we do so.
This is a pure play for a new dataset, that so far only Facebook has had access to.
You want to empty your shopping cart? You'll have to give us more data, thanks.
So far the facial recognition technology isn't very good, but it could get better fast if we take a selfie video every time we buy a can of coffee online.
If that happens, we won't need the GPS in our phones to give away our locations, because just by having faces we will be doing enough.
I've resigned myself to lots of data collection, but this selfie stuff is going too far.
key_insight: Tech companies frame new authentication features as conveniences, but they're actually mechanisms for extracting valuable biometric data that expands corporate and government surveillance capabilities.
